title: How tinctures work
subtitle: The basics of alcohol-and-water extraction
excerpt: A guide to how tinctures extract and preserve herb active compounds, why 40% spirit is the standard, the difference between folk method and pharmacopoeia method, and how to use a spirit extraction safely.
sourceNotes: the herbal pharmacopoeia, this spirit extraction monographs and dosing standards. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), this spirit extraction preparation methods and the folk versus standard approaches. the herbal reference, the use of standardised herbal tincture ratios for the traditional-use framework.

glossaryTerms.definition: The liquid used to extract herb constituents in a tincture. For most tinctures, the menstruum is water and alcohol (vodka, brandy, or pharmacopoeial spirit) in a specific ratio.
The spent herb material left after straining a tincture. The marc should be pressed firmly when straining; a significant portion of the finished tincture remains in the herb until squeezed out.

content.text: A tincture is an extraction of herb active compounds in a water-and-alcohol medium. The
menstruum

draws out a wider range of active compounds than water alone (the basis of an infusion or herbal decoction) because alcohol dissolves both water-soluble and fat-soluble active compounds. The alcohol also preserves the preparation for 2 years or more, removing the need to prepare fresh doses every day. These two properties, broader extraction and long shelf life, are why this tincture is the most common home preparation beyond simple infusions.
Why 40% spirit is the standard

Standard vodka, at 40% alcohol by volume (ABV), is the folk-method standard for two reasons. First, 40% is approximately the boundary where the menstruum extracts a useful range of both polar (water-soluble) and non-polar (fat-soluble) active compounds from most medicinal herbs. Below 25%, the extraction is largely water-based and many resinous or oily active compounds don't dissolve. Above 60%, the extraction becomes more resinous and the water-soluble, gel-forming active compounds are under-extracted. 40% is the practical middle ground.

Second, the standard herbal pharmacopoeia specifies 40-45% spirit for many common herbal tincture monographs, and most the herbal references that specify the menstruum use 40-60% spirit depending on the herb. Using plain vodka at 40% ABV puts the home preparation within this accepted range.
Folk method versus pharmacopoeia method

The folk method: fill a jar with dried herb, cover with spirit to a 2 cm margin above the herb, soaked 4-6 weeks, strain. This is a 1:5 herbal tincture by approximate weight-to-volume ratio (1 part dried herb to 5 parts spirit). It is not precisely standardised, but produces a consistent, usable preparation for home use.

The pharmacopoeia method: weigh the herb precisely, measure the spirit to achieve an exact weight-to-volume ratio (1:5, 1:3, or 1:2 depending on the herb's density), soaked for a specified time. This produces a reproducible preparation that matches the study form and gives more precise dosing. the standard herbal pharmacopoeia specifies the ratio and menstruum strength for each herb in its monographs. For home use, the folk method is standard; for therapeutic precision, the pharmacopoeia ratio is preferred.
The soaking period

Dried herb takes 4-6 weeks to fully give up its active compounds into the spirit. Freshly dried herb may give a good extraction at 4 weeks; older dried herb (approaching its 12-month shelf life) benefits from the full 6 weeks. Shaking the jar daily moves the herb through the liquid, prevents channelling, and keeps the extraction even. The visible sign of a complete extraction is that the herb has largely lost its colour to the spirit, and the spirit is dark and aromatic.
Pressing the marc
When straining a spirit extraction, the
marc

holds a significant fraction of the finished herbal tincture; pressing it firmly through muslin cloth typically recovers 15-25% more liquid than pouring alone. Press with both hands through the folded muslin until no more drips. Discard the spent marc; it has given everything it has to give.
Using a spirit extraction

Tinctures are typically dosed at 2-4 ml per dose (the standard dose range for most common tinctures), diluted in a small glass of water. The dilution is important: dropping a neat herbal tincture directly onto the tongue can irritate mucous membranes, and dilution helps distribute the dose. A 30 ml amber dropper bottle holds approximately 30 doses at 1 ml per dose or 15 doses at 2 ml.
Alcohol and tinctures

A standard 2-4 ml herbal tincture dose contains 0.8-1.6 ml of alcohol, which is less alcohol than a standard breath-freshener spray and orders of magnitude less than a glass of wine. This is not a meaningful intake for most people. However, readers who must avoid all alcohol, including those on metronidazole, disulfiram, or certain other medications, and those in recovery from alcohol dependence, should use glycerite or infusion preparations instead. Tinctures are also not appropriate for infants or young children.
Shelf life and storage

A properly made herbal tincture (dried herb, no moisture contamination, sealed amber glass, 40% spirit) keeps 2 years from the bottling date. Signs of spoilage are uncommon but include cloudiness (this spirit extraction should be dark but clear), unusual smell (rancid or fermented rather than herbal and alcoholic), or mould on the surface (this indicates contamination from undried herb or an improperly cleaned jar). Date-label every bottle at bottling.
